primary_diagnosis.name: Plantar wart (verruca plantaris) with mechanical irritation

pathophysiology_summary: A plantar wart is an HPV infection of the sole producing a hyperkeratotic papule; diagnostic features are disruption of the skin lines (dermatoglyphics) across the lesion and pinpoint thrombosed capillaries ('black dots') revealed on paring, with pain characteristically worse on direct vertical pressure (and less on side-to-side squeeze). Over a pressure point it acts like a 'stone in the shoe'. Discriminators: interrupted skin lines and black dots distinguish it from a corn/callus (skin lines preserved, avascular) and, on paring, from a retained foreign body (no foreign track); intact protective sensation with severe pain argues against a (painless) neuropathic diabetic ulcer; and the absence of surrounding cellulitis/pus/heat argues against a deep abscess. Management is mechanical relief now (paring the overlying keratin and an aperture/'doughnut' offloading pad) plus definitive treatment with topical salicylic acid (± cryotherapy/podiatry if it fails), avoiding pumicing (spreads virus) and using footwear/hygiene (thongs in public showers) to prevent reinfection. Safety-net for secondary bacterial infection.

differential.evidence_basis: Disrupted skin lines + black dots + pressure pain over a pressure point.
Preserved skin lines and no black dots/vascularity.
Paring would reveal a foreign track/material rather than wart architecture.
Screen — neuropathic ulcers are painless; deep infection/abscess shows heat/pus/fever/rest pain.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Recognise a plantar wart, exclude deep infection/abscess and neuropathic ulcer, and relieve the pressure (paring/offloading) plus definitive wart therapy.

differential.evidence_basis: Classic wart features; no deep-infection/abscess or neuropathic features.
Severe pressure pain with intact sensation and no heat/pus/fever — remains a safety-net.
Working diagnosis: paring + offloading pad + topical salicylic acid (± cryotherapy/podiatry); infection safety-net.

red_flags.description: Deep infection/abscess (surrounding hot/red swelling, thick pus, fever, continuous rest pain/throbbing)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would need drainage/antibiotics rather than wart treatment.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Missing the disrupted skin lines/black dots and treating as a simple corn.
Sending a benign pressure-painful wart to ED for suspected abscess/foreign body.
Advising pumicing that spreads the virus.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Advise paring by a clinician/podiatrist and an aperture offloading pad, topical salicylic acid (± cryotherapy), footwear/hygiene; review/urgent care only if deep-infection features.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Mislabelling as a corn/foreign body, or over-triaging for infection.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Plantar warts: topical salicylic acid ± cryotherapy; paring and offloading for pain; many resolve spontaneously; avoid pumicing (autoinoculation).
